critters.name: White-tailed Deer
critters.summary: Early-summer edge walkers now grazing hard while does get extra secretive and bucks start showing soft velvet growth.
critters.fact: White-tailed deer flash their white tails to signal danger and communicate with other deer nearby.
critters.activeFact: Right now, deer may be active near clover, trail edges, and openings, but does can vanish into cover fast if fawns are tucked nearby.

critters.name: Black Bear
critters.summary: Big woodland calorie vacuum now roaming greener woods where bugs, plants, and easy meals are all back on the menu.
critters.fact: Black bears have an excellent sense of smell, about seven times stronger than a bloodhound’s.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bear movement can increase near wet bottoms, berry patches forming up, rotting logs, and quiet feeding pockets with easy snacks.

critters.name: Coyote
critters.summary: Lean trail-route operator now hunting a busier landscape full of rabbits, rodents, nests, and young spring wildlife.
critters.fact: Coyotes use a variety of vocalizations, including howls and yips, to communicate with their pack.
critters.activeFact: Right now, coyotes may be working edges, two-tracks, and open lanes at dawn or dusk while hauling groceries back toward den areas.

critters.name: Red Fox
critters.summary: Rust-colored mouse sniper now balancing sharp hunting with the nonstop grocery run of kit season.
critters.fact: Red foxes use their bushy tails for balance and warmth during cold weather.
critters.activeFact: Right now, foxes may be seen crossing openings with prey or pausing in grass where rodents are moving through thicker green cover.

critters.name: Gray Fox
critters.summary: Brush-country ghost staying tucked in the green-up while quietly running its own family-and-food operation.
critters.fact: Gray foxes can climb trees to escape predators, unlike most other North American canids.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray fox activity is most likely along thick edges, brushy travel lanes, and shadowed timber where visibility is getting tighter.

critters.name: Bobcat
critters.summary: Silent timber stalker now using thicker cover and busier prey movement like the woods built it a hunting blind.
critters.fact: Bobcats can leap up to 10 feet high, helping them catch prey and escape predators efficiently.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bobcats may be working brush lines, trail crossings, and transition zones where young animals and small prey are moving more.

critters.name: Porcupine
critters.summary: Slow-motion tree tank now enjoying greener choices but still acting like bark is part of a balanced breakfast.
critters.fact: Porcupines have sharp quills that detach easily to protect them from predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, porcupines may be feeding in leafy trees, quiet edges, or woody patches where fresh growth is easy to reach.

critters.name: Beaver
critters.summary: Creek engineer still on duty, patching water problems like the wetlands keep submitting maintenance tickets.
critters.fact: Beavers can hold their breath underwater for up to 15 minutes while building dams and lodges.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fresh sticks, muddy slides, peeled branches, and newly packed dam spots are all signs the night crew has been busy.

critters.name: Muskrat
critters.summary: Pond commuter fully into warm-season shoreline life, popping around cattails like a tiny wetland boat captain.
critters.fact: Muskrats build dome-shaped lodges from vegetation, providing shelter and protection from predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, muskrats may be swimming pond edges, feeding on green aquatic growth, or slipping through cattails at dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Mink
critters.summary: Shoreline menace now patrolling frog-and-fish country with the confidence of something built for trouble.
critters.fact: Minks have waterproof fur that helps them swim and hunt aquatic prey efficiently.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mink activity may show along muddy banks, creek edges, pond margins, and narrow routes where prey is stacked close to water.

critters.name: Fisher
critters.summary: Deep-woods prowler moving through thicker green timber like it prefers the forest with the curtains closed.
critters.fact: Fishers use their sharp teeth to hunt porcupines, a rare skill among predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fishers may be mostly unseen in heavier woods, with trail cameras or tracks giving the best clue they passed through.

critters.name: River Otter
critters.summary: Water-slide troublemaker now working warmer creeks and ponds where fish, frogs, and mud all say yes.
critters.fact: River otters can close their ears and nostrils underwater to keep water out while swimming.
critters.activeFact: Right now, otters may be cruising channels, using muddy slides, and stirring up pond or creek edges where aquatic life is active.

critters.name: Eastern Gray Squirrel
critters.summary: Treetop chaos clerk now juggling leafy cover, young squirrels, feeder raids, and branch-to-branch nonsense.
critters.fact: Eastern Gray Squirrels communicate using tail flicks to warn others of nearby predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, gray squirrels are busy in leafy trees, around feeders, and along branches where young squirrels may start adding extra circus energy.

critters.name: American Red Squirrel
critters.summary: Tiny red alarm siren still defending the woods like every pine cone is protected by federal law.
critters.fact: American Red Squirrels fiercely defend their food caches during winter to survive harsh conditions.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red squirrels may be extra loud around conifers, brush piles, and feeding areas where they refuse to share the stage.

critters.name: Chipmunk
critters.summary: Pocket rocket fully back in summer traffic, stuffing cheeks and treating every log like a launch ramp.
critters.fact: Chipmunks have cheek pouches to carry food back to their burrows for storage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chipmunks are darting across trails, logs, and rock edges while collecting food and ducking into burrow entrances.

critters.name: Snowshoe Hare
critters.summary: Northern hare now mostly swapped into brown gear while thickening cover finally starts doing its job again.
critters.fact: Snowshoe hares change fur color from brown in summer to white in winter for seasonal camouflage.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hares may be harder to spot as leaves and brush fill in, especially where brown coats blend into the early-summer understory.

critters.name: Eastern Cottontail
critters.summary: Brush-edge hopper now feeding through green abundance while keeping one foot near the panic button.
critters.fact: Eastern Cottontails can run up to 18 miles per hour to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cottontails may be feeding along lawns, trail edges, clover patches, and brush piles, especially near dawn and dusk.

critters.name: Raccoon
critters.summary: Night-shift snack burglar now enjoying peak buffet season around water, bugs, eggs, and anything poorly secured.
critters.fact: Raccoons have highly sensitive front paws that help them identify food by touch in the dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, raccoons may be working pond edges, creeks, feeders, campsites, and trash zones with criminal little hand energy.

critters.name: Opossum
critters.summary: Warm-night wanderer finally in its comfort zone, patrolling edges for bugs, scraps, and cleanup duty.
critters.fact: Opossums are the only North American marsupials, carrying their babies in pouches like kangaroos.
critters.activeFact: Right now, opossums may be active around sheds, brush, compost areas, road edges, and camp margins during warmer nights.

critters.name: Striped Skunk
critters.summary: Striped lawn inspector now rooting through soft ground like every grub owes back rent.
critters.fact: Striped skunks can spray a foul-smelling liquid up to 10 feet to deter predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, skunks may be nosing through lawns, trail edges, damp soil, and field margins where bug life is picking up.

critters.name: Flying Squirrel
critters.summary: After-dark glider running silent treetop errands while the rest of the woods sleeps through the good stuff.
critters.fact: Flying squirrels glide up to 150 feet using a special flap of skin called a patagium.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nighttime cameras, feeders, cavities, or nest boxes may catch them moving quietly through leafy tree lanes.

critters.name: Little Brown Bat
critters.summary: Small bug vacuum now working warmer dusk skies where the insect buffet finally has decent hours.
critters.fact: Little Brown Bats use echolocation to navigate and catch insects in complete darkness.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mild nights may bring feeding loops over ponds, clearings, trails, and tree lines where mosquitoes and small flies are moving.

critters.name: Big Brown Bat
critters.summary: Tough dusk hunter now cruising warm evenings like pest control with wings and zero paperwork.
critters.fact: Big Brown Bats use echolocation to navigate and catch insects, consuming hundreds nightly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may appear near buildings, lights, woodland openings, and pond edges when warmer evenings bring bugs up.

critters.name: Barred Owl
critters.summary: Deep-woods night boss still calling through leafy timber while young mouths may be keeping the schedule busy.
critters.fact: Barred Owls have distinctive hoots that sound like “Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you all?”
critters.activeFact: Right now, calls can still roll through the woods at dusk or dawn, but hunting and family duty may be taking center stage.

critters.name: Great Horned Owl
critters.summary: Early-nesting heavyweight now deep into owl-parent mode while fledglings test the edges of independence.
critters.fact: Great Horned Owls can rotate their heads up to 270 degrees to spot prey in the dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, adults may be guarding and feeding young around nest territories while the youngsters practice being terrifying fluff with talons.

critters.name: Blue Jay
critters.summary: Blue feathered security department now defending nest space with a volume knob stuck on rude.
critters.fact: Blue Jays can mimic hawk calls to scare away other birds from feeders.
critters.activeFact: Right now, blue jays may be loud, aggressive, and extremely interested in anything moving near their trees or feeder routes.

critters.name: Northern Cardinal
critters.summary: Red morning announcer still posted in the brush, singing like the day does not start without approval.
critters.fact: Male Northern Cardinals sing to defend territory and attract mates with their bright red feathers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cardinals are active around shrubs, feeders, thickets, and woodland edges where nesting pairs stay busy.

critters.name: Black-capped Chickadee
critters.summary: Tiny cavity-crew boss now deep in nest season, bouncing around like a feathered project manager.
critters.fact: Black-capped Chickadees can remember hundreds of seed hiding spots using impressive spatial memory.
critters.activeFact: Right now, chickadees may be moving between nest holes, branches, and feeders while hauling tiny groceries through the green-up.

critters.name: Tufted Titmouse
critters.summary: Gray little loudspeaker now working leafy branches with nest-season confidence and unnecessary commentary.
critters.fact: Tufted Titmice mimic other birds’ calls to communicate and defend their territory.
critters.activeFact: Right now, titmice may be calling, checking cavities, and moving through edge trees where insect activity is rising.

critters.name: White-breasted Nuthatch
critters.summary: Upside-down bark runner still treating tree trunks like sidewalks while nest season keeps it busy.
critters.fact: White-breasted Nuthatches can climb down trees headfirst, unlike most other birds.
critters.activeFact: Right now, nuthatches may be busy on mature trunks, slipping headfirst through leafy woods and carrying food toward nest sites.

critters.name: Downy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Small bark drummer now working nest duty and bug patrol with tiny hammer precision.
critters.fact: Downy Woodpeckers tap tree bark to find insects and create nesting holes.
critters.activeFact: Right now, downies may be tapping, calling, and carrying food through trees as nesting activity keeps the tempo up.

critters.name: Hairy Woodpecker
critters.summary: Bigger timber hammer still sounding off from mature trees while the bug hunt gets serious.
critters.fact: Hairy Woodpeckers use their long tongues to extract insects from deep tree bark crevices.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hairy woodpeckers may be heard in older timber, working trunks and feeding young or defending nesting space.

critters.name: Pileated Woodpecker
critters.summary: Giant forest carpenter now tearing into summer bug season like the trees insulted his family.
critters.fact: Pileated Woodpeckers create large rectangular holes when searching for ants in dead trees.
critters.activeFact: Right now, loud calls, heavy knocking, and fresh chips at tree bases can reveal where pileateds are working the woods.

critters.name: Red-bellied Woodpecker
critters.summary: Vocal woodland hammerhead now running nest season like every tree needs a complaint department.
critters.fact: Red-bellied Woodpeckers drum loudly to communicate and establish territory, not just to find food.
critters.activeFact: Right now, they may be calling, scolding, feeding, and working tree trunks around nest areas and feeder routes.

critters.name: Mourning Dove
critters.summary: Soft-voiced twig minimalist now proving again that “good enough” is apparently a nest strategy.
critters.fact: Mourning Doves can fly up to 55 miles per hour to escape predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, paired doves may be cooing, carrying twigs, sitting nests, or making questionable construction choices in plain sight.

critters.name: American Goldfinch
critters.summary: Brightening seed specialist now looking more summer-ready but still waiting for peak nesting timing.
critters.fact: American Goldfinches molt twice yearly, changing from bright yellow breeding plumage to duller winter feathers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, males are getting brighter, flocks are lively, and nesting urgency is building but not at full blast yet.

critters.name: Dark-eyed Junco
critters.summary: Cool-weather ground feeder mostly fading from the main stage as summer birds take over the playlist.
critters.fact: Dark-eyed Juncos are known as “snowbirds” because they often appear during winter across North America.
critters.activeFact: Right now, juncos may be scarce or moving out of regular feeder scenes as warmer-season birds dominate the ground traffic.

critters.name: House Finch
critters.summary: Social little singer now fully in neighborhood-radio mode, broadcasting from feeders and brush tops.
critters.fact: House Finches are native to the western US but spread across North America after escaping captivity.
critters.activeFact: Right now, house finches may be paired, nesting, singing, and making steady feeder visits with summer confidence.

critters.name: Purple Finch
critters.summary: Rosy woodland singer still possible in northern cover, drifting through like it enjoys being hard to schedule.
critters.fact: Purple Finches have a sweet, warbling song often described as a mix between a robin and a house finch.
critters.activeFact: Right now, purple finches may show up around forest edges and feeders, but appearances can still feel brief and unpredictable.

critters.name: Wild Turkey
critters.summary: Strut-season drama crew now shifting toward hens nesting while gobblers still act like show business is alive.
critters.fact: Wild turkeys can run up to 25 miles per hour and fly short distances at 55 miles per hour.
critters.activeFact: Right now, hens may be secretive in cover, while toms can still appear in openings acting like the season has one encore left.

critters.name: Ruffed Grouse
critters.summary: Forest drummer easing off the loud solo while thick cover becomes nursery and hiding space.
critters.fact: Ruffed Grouse produce a distinctive drumming sound by rapidly beating air with their fan-shaped tail feathers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, drumming may still happen, but quiet hens and tiny chicks may be tucked into leafy ground cover.

critters.name: Sandhill Crane
critters.summary: Tall marsh dinosaur now deep into nesting or colt-raising season with maximum voice and minimum subtlety.
critters.fact: Sandhill Cranes perform synchronized dancing to strengthen pair bonds and communicate with each other.
critters.activeFact: Right now, cranes may be guarding wetland territories, feeding in open areas, or moving with young nearby if nesting succeeded.

critters.name: American Crow
critters.summary: Black-feathered property auditor now running nest-season surveillance on everything suspicious, edible, or shiny.
critters.fact: American Crows use tools, like sticks, to help them gather food in the wild.
critters.activeFact: Right now, crows may be loud, watchful, and quick to mob hawks, owls, or anything they decide is bad news.

critters.name: Common Raven
critters.summary: Big woodland trickster cruising over green timber like it knows the punchline before anyone else hears the joke.
critters.fact: Common Ravens can mimic sounds, including human speech, and use complex problem-solving skills in the wild.
critters.activeFact: Right now, ravens may be traveling in pairs or family groups, calling over timber, and working familiar routes with purpose.

critters.name: Red-tailed Hawk
critters.summary: Airborne landlord now tied tight to nest territory while every careless field mouse is living dangerously.
critters.fact: Red-tailed Hawks use their keen eyesight to spot prey from over a mile away.
critters.activeFact: Right now, red-tails may be perched near nest areas, circling fields, and bringing prey back toward hungry young.

critters.name: Cooper’s Hawk
critters.summary: Fast woodland missile now threading leafy trees where songbirds suddenly remember fear exists.
critters.fact: Cooper’s Hawks are agile flyers, often hunting birds through dense trees with quick, sharp maneuvers.
critters.activeFact: Right now, quick low flights through trees may mean a Cooper’s hawk is hunting or patrolling near an active nest.

critters.name: Mallard Duck
critters.summary: Classic pond duck now moving through early-summer water while hens keep nesting and brood plans quiet.
critters.fact: Male mallards have a distinctive glossy green head that helps them attract mates during the breeding season.
critters.activeFact: Right now, mallards may be loafing, feeding, nesting, or leading ducklings through shallow pond edges and flooded vegetation.

critters.name: Wood Duck
critters.summary: Flashy backwater duck now slipping through green pockets where ducklings may already be tiny chaos boats.
critters.fact: Wood Ducks nest in tree cavities and are one of the most colorful North American waterfowl species.
critters.activeFact: Right now, wood ducks may be hiding in sheltered pond edges, flooded timber, and quiet bends where young broods stay protected.

critters.name: Canada Goose
critters.summary: Pond security staff now upgraded to gosling protection mode, which somehow made them even less reasonable.
critters.fact: Canada Geese mate for life and can fly thousands of miles during their annual migration.
critters.activeFact: Right now, goose families may be grazing near ponds and fields, with adults watching every step like unpaid bouncers.

critters.name: Great Blue Heron
critters.summary: Long-legged fish thief now hunting shallow water where frogs and small fish are having a very bad season.
critters.fact: Great Blue Herons stand still for long periods to patiently catch fish with their sharp beaks.
critters.activeFact: Right now, herons may be stalking pond edges, creek shallows, and marsh margins with that frozen-statue patience.

critters.name: Painted Turtle
critters.summary: Solar-powered pond pebble now basking hard and getting ready for nesting-season road nonsense.
critters.fact: Painted turtles can absorb oxygen through their skin while underwater, helping them stay submerged longer.
critters.activeFact: Right now, turtles may be stacked on logs, crossing warm banks, or showing up near sandy or soft nesting spots.

critters.name: Snapping Turtle
critters.summary: Swamp tank now more active and entering the season where road crossings get real weird real fast.
critters.fact: Snapping turtles have powerful jaws that can snap shut with incredible force to catch prey quickly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, snapping turtles may be moving through shallows, crossing banks, or wandering away from water in search of egg-laying spots.

critters.name: Bluegill
critters.summary: Little panfish brawler now sliding toward bed season where shallow circles become serious business.
critters.fact: Bluegills can change color to blend into their surroundings, helping them avoid predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bluegill may be staging or building beds in warm shallows, especially near firm bottom, weeds, or protected edges.

critters.name: Largemouth Bass
critters.summary: Shallow-water bruiser now in prime spawn-zone attitude, guarding beds and ruining small-fish plans.
critters.fact: Largemouth bass can expand their mouths wide enough to swallow prey nearly half their body size.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bass may be holding tight to beds, shallow cover, or nearby drop-offs depending on water temperature and spawning stage.

critters.name: Yellow Perch
critters.summary: Striped school fish now past the cold-water rush and sliding into warmer feeding lanes.
critters.fact: Yellow Perch have distinctive vertical stripes and are popular among anglers for their tasty, white flesh.
critters.activeFact: Right now, perch may be schooling near weeds, deeper edges, or soft nearshore structure where food is collecting.

critters.name: Northern Pike
critters.summary: Toothy green missile now past early spawning and back to ambush duty in weedy lanes.
critters.fact: Northern Pike can grow over four feet long and use ambush tactics to catch prey quickly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, pike may be lurking near weeds, flooded cover, creek mouths, and shallow ambush spots with bad intentions.

critters.name: Spring Peeper
critters.summary: Tiny noise machine still in the chorus mix, though the big frogs are starting to steal some thunder.
critters.fact: Spring Peepers are tiny frogs known for their loud, high-pitched calls that signal early spring.
critters.activeFact: Right now, peepers may still be calling from wet woods and low spots, especially after rain or on mild humid evenings.

critters.name: American Bullfrog
critters.summary: Big pond bass drum finally warming up, ready to add deep complaints to the night soundtrack.
critters.fact: American Bullfrogs can produce loud, deep calls that carry over 1 mile to attract mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bullfrogs may be starting to call from pond edges, warm shallows, and weedy corners on calmer evenings.

critters.name: Green Frog
critters.summary: Shoreline banjo frog now getting louder as warm water turns pond edges into amphibian headquarters.
critters.fact: Green frogs have a distinctive ridge running from their eyes down their backs, helping identify them easily.
critters.activeFact: Right now, green frogs may be calling or sitting along sunny banks, weedy edges, and shallow water where insects and cover are close.

critters.name: Leopard Frog
critters.summary: Spotted grass jumper now roaming wet edges where spring moisture and thicker cover give it options.
critters.fact: Leopard frogs can jump up to 20 times their body length to escape predators quickly.
critters.activeFact: Right now, leopard frogs may be spotted near wet grass, puddles, marsh edges, and trails close to damp cover.

critters.name: Garter Snake
critters.summary: Sun-powered noodle now fully back on patrol, hunting frogs, worms, and anything slow enough to regret it.
critters.fact: Garter snakes can survive in cold climates by slowing their metabolism during winter hibernation.
critters.activeFact: Right now, garter snakes may be basking on trails, hunting near wet areas, or slipping through grass where frogs are active.

critters.name: Water Snake
critters.summary: Bank-side attitude rope now warming up for full shoreline season with frogs and fish on the menu.
critters.fact: Water snakes can stay underwater for up to an hour by holding their breath.
critters.activeFact: Right now, water snakes may be basking near pond edges, sliding into shallows, or holding close to sunny banks.

critters.name: Eastern Milk Snake
critters.summary: Secretive cover snake now moving warmer edges while rodents make poor life decisions nearby.
critters.fact: Eastern Milk Snakes can mimic rattlesnakes by vibrating their tails to scare predators.
critters.activeFact: Right now, milk snakes may be found around warm cover, wood piles, old boards, and brushy edges on mild days.

critters.name: Monarch Butterfly
critters.summary: Orange traveler finally becoming more realistic as milkweed rises and migration pushes farther north.
critters.fact: Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles to wintering sites in Mexico and California.
critters.activeFact: Right now, monarchs may start appearing more often where milkweed and early nectar sources are available.

critters.name: Swallowtail Butterfly
critters.summary: Big-winged summer showoff now entering the chat as warm afternoons and flowers finally cooperate.
critters.fact: Swallowtail butterflies can mimic toxic species to avoid predators, enhancing their chances of survival.
critters.activeFact: Right now, swallowtails may be cruising sunny openings, garden edges, trail margins, and flowering plants on calm warm days.

critters.name: Dragonfly
critters.summary: Aerial pond patrol now beginning to emerge, trading underwater childhood for mosquito-control air superiority.
critters.fact: Dragonflies can fly in six directions: upward, downward, forward, backward, and side to side.
critters.activeFact: Right now, early dragonflies may be appearing around ponds, marshes, and sunny water edges where insects are rising.

critters.name: Damselfly
critters.summary: Delicate wetland flyer now starting to show above the reeds like the pond unlocked a new level.
critters.fact: Damselflies can fold their wings along their bodies, unlike dragonflies that keep wings spread out.
critters.activeFact: Right now, damselflies may begin hovering near shoreline plants, reeds, and calm water on warm still days.

critters.name: Firefly
critters.summary: Summer lantern crew getting closer to showtime while the leaf litter crew finishes charging the batteries.
critters.fact: Fireflies use bioluminescent light to attract mates and communicate in the dark.
critters.activeFact: Right now, fireflies may still be mostly hidden, but warmer early-June nights mean glow season is creeping closer.

critters.name: Honeybee
critters.summary: Flower-shift worker now clocking in across blossoms like the meadow finally opened for business.
critters.fact: Honeybees communicate by dancing to share the location of flowers with their hive mates.
critters.activeFact: Right now, honeybees may be steady on blooming flowers, clover, garden plants, and sunny edges whenever weather cooperates.

critters.name: Bumblebee
critters.summary: Fuzzy pollinator crew now expanding beyond queen scouts as early colonies start getting real.
critters.fact: Bumblebees can buzz-pollinate flowers by vibrating their flight muscles to release pollen efficiently.
critters.activeFact: Right now, bumblebees may be common around blossoms, clover, shrubs, and garden edges where pollen and nectar are reliable.

critters.name: Grasshopper
critters.summary: Summer hopper finally warming up from bench duty as grasses thicken and sunny edges get crunchy.
critters.fact: Grasshoppers have strong hind legs that allow them to jump over 20 times their body length.
critters.activeFact: Right now, young grasshoppers may start appearing in sunny grass, trail edges, and dry openings as early summer builds.
